title: Building a temporary hurdle pen
subtitle: How to set up a working pen from hurdles for routine handling, catching, and treatment without permanent infrastructure.
excerpt: A hurdle pen takes 15 minutes to set up and turns an open field into a manageable working space. The geometry of the pen determines whether it works: a badly angled funnel sends animals back into the field; a well-designed one moves them into a handling race or catch pen without a chase. Getting the angles right is the main skill.
sourceNotes: NSA Sheep Farmer: portable penning systems. AHDB Beef and Lamb: low-stress sheep handling. RSPCA: moving and handling livestock.

glossaryTerms.definition: A portable, lightweight fencing panel used to create temporary enclosures for livestock. Traditional hurdles are woven hazel; modern versions are galvanised steel or aluminium bar stock with a hook-and-pin connection system that allows them to be linked at various angles. A standard sheep hurdle is approximately 2.4 m long and 90 cm high.

The arrangement of hurdles that narrows from a large holding area into a smaller race or catch pen, guiding animals in one direction without a dead end that causes them to turn back. The narrowing angle should be gradual: a sudden 90-degree turn at the entrance to a race causes animals to baulk. The classic arrangement is a large V-shape narrowing to a race entrance of 60 to 80 cm.

content.text: pen is the most versatile infrastructure on a small mixed holding. It works for sheep, goats, pigs on range, and poultry round-up. The design principles are the same across species: use a wall or fence as one side, create a holding area large enough that the animals feel they have space, and funnel toward a catch point.

Start with the holding area: a large rectangular pen large enough to hold the whole group without crowding. Crowded animals in a holding pen cannot be moved as a group because there is no space for natural flock movement. For a group of 10 sheep, the holding area needs at least 20 to 25 square metres. From the far end of the holding pen, create the
using two hurdles angled inward to form a V. The narrow end of the V opens into a smaller catch pen or race.
Use an existing wall or fence as the back wall of the catch pen. This reduces the number of hurdles needed and gives a solid surface behind the animals when they are caught.
Do not face the funnel entrance toward the gate the animals came through. They will push back toward the familiar exit instead of entering the funnel.
Orient the pen so the catch pen end does not face directly into bright sun: animals move toward light, not away from it.

Bring the whole group into the holding area calmly, then close the gate behind you before applying pressure. A handler at the back of the holding area applies gentle, intermittent pressure toward the funnel. One or two animals will enter the funnel and the rest will follow as a group. Avoid driving fast or loudly: a group that has been startled presses into corners and does not move as a unit. Work slowly, use pauses, and let the animals' natural flocking behaviour do most of the work.
